Here's how you could do this using the Unity container: 1. Firstly, add necessary namespaces: ``` using Microsoft.Practices.Unity; using System.Net.Http; ``` 2. Then modify the registration of `IMyClass` in your container to use a singleton lifestyle for HttpClient: ```csharp var container = new UnityContainer(); container.RegisterType<IMyClass, MyClass>(new InjectionConstructor( new ResolvedParameter<HttpClient>(context => { if (context.ResolveOptions.IsRecursionDetected) return null; return context.Existing < IMyclass >().Value.Client; // Reuse the existing HttpClient instance }))); container.RegisterType<IMyClass>(new TransientLifetimeManager()); ``` 3. Now when you need to use your class, it will always have access to a single `HttpClient`: ```csharp var myClAss = container.Resolve<IMyClass>(); myClass.SomeMethodNeedingClient(myClass.Client); ``` In the code above, the `ResolvedParameter` is used for injecting an existing instance of HttpClient. The check `if (context.ResolveOptions.IsRecursionDetected)` prevents infinite recursion in case when no instances are registered yet. Note: It's important to note that although this approach will keep a single instance of HttpClient across your application, it might not be the best way to handle HTTP requests as they can be long-lived and consume resources. Consider using `HttpClientFactory` for managing multiple HttpClients with different lifetimes if necessary

This is then called from within any action of subclasses as needed by passing in the specific method name to be executed on `$manager`. This approach reduces code duplication and makes it easier to maintain if changes need to be made, such as changing how exceptions are handled or what should happen when an exception occurs. Note: The `getManager()` function is added for good practice. This ensures that each request gets a fresh instance of the manager object (assuming MyManager isn't thread-safe) which can prevent potential issues with shared state between requests if you're using Symfony's default controller instantiation behavior, or similar situations where multiple simultaneous requests might be running methods on your class at once. The model we consider here consists of $N$ identical particles moving freely on $\mathbb{R}^d$, but interacting via a pairwise delta-function potential and an additional three-body interaction that is also supported at zero distance. The mean field limit for such systems, as $N \to \infty$ with the interparticle spacing tending to 0 appropriately, leads us naturally to study the Gross-Pitaevskii hierarchy. This hierarchy provides a description of the evolution in terms of marginal density matrices of increasing order; it is an infinite system of coupled partial differential equations. Our main result states that for initial data which are factorizable (i.e., they can be written as products of single-particle quantum state functions), solutions to this hierarchy converge, as $N \to \infty$, towards a solution of the defQNLS. In particular, we show existence and uniqueness of such limiting dynamics. The proof is based on several technical advances over previous work in [2]–[4]. Firstly, our treatment allows for three-particle interactions without restricting to repulsive interactions; this requires new estimates compared with those used previously when only two-body interactions are present. Secondly, we extend the method of Klainerman-Machedon from $d=3$ down to $d=1$, and then further generalize it in order to handle three-particle interactions. The outline of our paper is as follows: In Section 2, we define precisely the model under consideration; in particular, this includes a discussion about factorizability. Then, starting from these microscopic equations (Section 3), we derive macroscopic dynamics for which mean field limit holds true and show that they converge to solutions of defQNLS. We conclude with some remarks on future directions: Firstly, it would be interesting to see if our methods can also handle other types of three-particle interactions. Secondly, one could try to extend the techniques developed here to study systems in higher dimensions or more particles (e.g., four-body interactions). Finally, another possible direction is to investigate whether similar results hold for fermions with 3-particle interactions. [1] Hainzl, Jürg; Seiringer R. or rotation of optically manipulated isotropic dielectric micro-particles using the unique properties of an Archimedean spiral structure embedded with metallic nanoparticles. This setup exploits localized surface plasmon resonances (LSPRs) to generate strong optical near fields, which are capable of exerting forces on nearby particles. The experimental design involves fabricating a nanostructured metal-coated dielectric substrate in the form of an Archimedes spiral patterned at sub-wavelength scales. When illuminated with light tuned close to its plasmonic resonance frequency, this structure generates highly localized and intense optical near fields along specific regions of the spiral arms due to enhanced electromagnetic field confinement. The selective trapping or rotation mechanism is achieved by exploiting differences in particle size, refractive index contrast between particles and surrounding medium, as well as their initial positions relative to these high-intensity hotspots. The gradient forces arising from spatial variations in optical intensity can trap microparticles at specific locations along the spiral arms where field enhancement peaks. Moreover, due to asymmetrical distribution of near-field intensities around each hotspot on a given arm segment and phase differences induced by light propagation through different parts of the structure, particles experience torque. This results in rotational motion when trapped within these regions if their size is appropriately matched with the spatial periodicity of enhanced fields along spiral arms. A comparison between industrialised and developing countries. There is no reason to believe that periodontal diseases in industrialised and developing countries are in principle different. That is, not in the sense that the problem is caused by a different set of periodontal diseases, with different micro-organisms and a different natural history, needing different treatment and prevention. However, the prevalence, severity, and the way periodontal diseases are managed and perceived are different, and are influenced by a variety of factors, including the level of economic and social development, the availability and the level of the health care system, and the health and hygiene habits of the population. In industrialised countries, the prevalence of periodontal diseases, including periodontal pockets and clinical attachment loss, has been decreasing, and the population has, on the whole, been retaining more of their natural teeth. This has been the result of more widespread and more effective health and hygiene habits, more effective and more widespread health and hygiene education, and more effective and more widespread periodontal and dental health and hygiene services. OPINION OF THE COURT The Appellant, William Bell ("Bell"), was convicted of being a Habitual Motor Vehicle Offender and sentenced accordingly. The trial court found that he had accumulated the requisite number of prior offenses to be designated as an habitual offender under Tennessee law (T.C.A., 59-104). On appeal before this Court is whether or not Bell's sentence was properly imposed given his past criminal history. The State contends, and we agree with their position that based on evidence presented at trial regarding the number of prior convictions against him for driving offenses in violation Tennessee motor vehicle laws over a period spanning several years; it meets statutory requirements to qualify as an habitual offender under T.C.A. 59-104(a)(2). In conclusion, we affirm Bell's conviction and sentence on appeal. AFFIRMED Delivered by: Andrew Joanne Macree Watts Judge Clerk of the Court: Bernie Weinman

In my home growing-up I was exposed to Italian cuisine first-hand from both parents who hailed originally as 'pasta makers' or pastaio in our mother tongue. Our family recipes were passed down mainly by oral tradition, which is quite common among Italians; every grandma and great-grandmother would have their special recipe they swore upon. These tales of traditional dishes often began with the story behind each dish: 'This soup was my grandmother's favourite', or a tale about how an aunt managed to make this risotto despite living in Italy without rice for many years. Our family meals were communal events where stories, laughter and lessons from our day would be shared over hearty plates of pasta. The recipes themselves are nothing fancy but their authenticity makes them the essence on what Italian cuisine stands upon today – fresh ingredients like tomatoes, basil or olive oil that when combined in just a simple way bring out an explosion taste. Food for us Italians is not only about nourishment; it's also deeply embedded into our cultural heritage and identity. Each dish holds stories of its origins: the comforting warmth from Nonna’s lasagna reminds me my family, while balsamic vinegar sauce carries tales of Northern Italy which was parted by time.
